As per this report from Economic Times, Tata is readying a Micro SUV to compete with likes of Mahindra KUV1OO & Maruti Ignis. Launch likely in 2020 or 2021.


Quote:
Tata Motors has advanced the launch of the X445, or the Hornbill, by a year from the previous plan of 2022.
Quote:
Mayank Pareek, president of the passenger vehicle division at Tata Motors, declined to comment on the company’s product plans, but said the opportunity for small SUVs was huge. “People who have been purchasing hatchbacks for years will be looking for an upgrade. India sells over 1million hatchbacks every year. Almost 58% of the owners replace their car every six years (and for them) the micro or small SUV becomes a good alternative,” he said.
Quote:
this model was initially to arrive in 2022, but Tata Motors has preponed the launch after relooking the segment.
The segment was pioneered by Mahindra’s KUV 100. Maruti Suzuki’s Ignis too falls in the same price range.
Quote:
The Tata Hornbill will ride on the Advanced Modular Platform, which will debut in the Tata 45X in CY2019 and underpin a mid-size sedan the following year.



Quote:
Though Tata Motors is working in various directions to meet the upcoming BS6 norms, a turbocharged petrol engine would make the X445 a costly affair considering its rivals and positioning. Instead, TML could offer the X445 with a 1.2-litre naturally aspirated petrol engine, maybe a refitted Tata Tiago’s unit or a newly developed unit, along with manual and automated versions of the TA65 5-speed transmission.

Tata Motors did give a glimpse of the Hornbill in their 45X and H5X launch video. There you can see it emanating from the 45X.


http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IlK0RKoJF-4

Tata Motors have to play it really smart here. This is like jumping into a segment which has tasted only lukewarm success. They should not benchmark the Hornbill against the Ignis or the Kwid as it could lead to cost cutting to undercut them and they would have to compromise on quality which is where they are improving leaps and bounds. Instead, they could try to consider the Hornbill as a product which focuses on their Impact 2.0 design and AMP atchitecture and slot it right below the Nexon. Well, you might argue then it will obviously be an Ignis rival, but Tata Motors developing a product with their own new design philosophy in mind will more likely create a better car which could open a fresh new proper 'micro suv segment' than simply developing a car to compete with the KUV100 or the Ignis which are just vague attempts at anything to a 'Micro SUV' which they are trying to be desperately.

Tata Motors can really use this opportunity to create the first proper 'Micro SUV' segment by focusing on the main elements that fit the SUV bill.

- Proper SUV stance.
- High ground clearance
- Engines that focus on high torque output than efficiency. People who want efficiency can look at the Tiago/Tigor.
- Commanding view from the driver seat
- Space (may be the new AMP platform could help here)
- Larger 15 inches high profile tyres. (Maybe atleast standard on higher trims)

These are values that both the Ignis and the KUV100 simply lack.

Too late? This is the biggest misconception we seem to have on Teambhp. "Too late" is a term used very often here to describe latecomers but let's look at where some of the early birds are.

1. Sumo pioneered MUV segment and was all the rage in early 90s to mid-90s. Then Qualis came and later Innova and we know where Sumo is today.

2. Safari was the first diesel SUV. Again completely eclipsed by latecomer Scorpio.

3. Indigo, the first sub-4 metre sedan. All gone now thanks to later arrival Dzire.

4. Indica, first diesel hatchback. lost to Swift diesel.

5. Duster. First mini SUV. Lost big time to 3 years late coming Creta, and now to Seltos.

6. Ecosport. First sub-4 metre mini-SUV. Lost big time to Brezza.

7. E20, Veritos and other such underpowered, low-range EVs form Mahindra and even TML. They will capitulate to Tata's own Nexon, MG ZS and Kona.

Early to market always has advantages. Late to market mean higher barrier of entry and lost opportunity.

There was a company called Hero Motors under another arm of Hero Group(Hero Motors, not Present Hero Motocorp). Hero motors best known for Hero Puch and few other mopeds, scooters). Honda had tie up with Kinetic for scooters, best product, Kinetic Honda and Bikes with Hero Honda, CD100, CD 100SS, CD Delux and infamous Splendor.

Honda wanted to have its way. Even Suzuki and Yamaha wanted their own ways knowing that Indian market is huge. Every one started breaking ties.

Lets look at the history. Honda first severed ties with Kinetic. Kinetic never recovered. Because Kinetic did not have a plan B.

But severing ties with Hero Honda was a big risk for Honda. So what they did was something shrewd. They started own company HMSI and started making first geared scooter, which was a flop but automatic scooter Activa clicked. They did not break ties with Hero Group until Honda was fully established with Activa product line and they had setup sales and show rooms every where.

Meanwhile, Hero Motors, a part of Hero group had no relationship with Honda. They could have started a equally good product similar to Activa, either their own or some other foreign technology and sold along with showroom which were selling Hero Puch 3G and other scooters. No, They did not!! Hero group chairman repeatedly kept saying Honda will never part ways with them. And finally when relationship was broken, Hero had to start their own R&D from scratch and had to pay royalty to Honda for few years even after break up, for the engine they were using in the product line.

Meanwhile Activa had reached selling over 3 lakhs per month. Hero came out Maestro, which was almost similar to Activa but failed. TVS came out with Wego, but failed. Only TVS Jupiter came close with decent numbers but even today Activa is a well established brand selling 3 lakh units a month.

If some one tells me that only Honda can manufacture product like Activa and not Hero motors or TVS, then I will say, go and drink a cup of tea. Hero Motors, TVS and our own bajaj could have manufactured a product far better than Activa. But they failed to see the new product coming in their own backyard and dismissed and lost the market share.

If hero Motors had woken up when Honda was selling at around 30,000 or 40,000 units month of Activa, probably they would have started a development of product and would have launched when Activa was selling around a lakh per month. No! They waited till marriage was broken, took over one year and then launched Maestro, when Activa was selling over 3 lakhs a month consistently for years. These companies Bajaj, TVS, Hero virtually gave the market share on a silver platter to Honda, Free... Period.

Maruti Omini: You call it unsafe, junk, tin can, but sold minimum 5000 units month, No advertisement, No promotion, Nothing. Not even design changes for years, until safety standards kicked in and the product was removed from sales.

Wagon R: This vehicle never had competition, Tall boy design, Call it unsafe, ugly, what ever. Always sold over 10,000 units a month. Never a flop. No one could come with a comparable product.

Swift and Swift Dzire diesel: What was Tata doing when these products were entering market?

Early to market always has its merits. You come first, make mistakes, customers will forgive you. Because even they don't know about that. But if you are second or third, immediately people will start comparing, bench marking. Meanwhile, the first one who came early, would have recovered the cost of development, dyes, manufacturing capital expenses, will now reduce price to kill the competition. Now the second or third guy will not only try to capture market share but also fight price war and recover his development expenses.


This is why Tesla is successful even where established players are failing.
This is why Maruti is successful where Tata and Mahindra failed.

Manufacturing is a whole different ball game. It's business and it's brutal..

The Tata HBX Concept is based on Tata's "Agile Light Flexible Advanced" Architecture (ALFA ARC). It is an extension of the H2X Concept that was displayed at the Geneva Motor Show in March 2019. The car is being readied for production. Test mules of the vehicle have already been spotted on our roads.

The HBX Concept is beefed up to make it rugged-looking:


Front end is similar to the Harrier with DRLs located just below the bonnet and headlamps placed on the bumper. Auxiliary lamps are located on the grille. Silver skid plate is huge:


Body cladding and silver skid plate on the sides. Rear door handle is located on the C-Pillar. All pillars are blacked out. Roof rack carries spare wheel and two jerry cans:


Rear end is influenced by the Nexon. LED lamps look funky. Like the front, there is a huge silver skid plate:


What is becoming a feature on most Tata cars, there is an animal marked on the car:


Chunky tyres add to the rugged look:


"Tata Motors Design" lettering on the side body cladding:


Interiors feature a black dashboard with copper inserts. Leather wrapped steering wheel has a copper insert and contrast stitching. Don't miss the rhinoceros on the spoke of the steering wheel:


Doorpad has the same theme as the dashboard:


Black upholstery with copper stitching and highlights:


A look at the rear bench. Notice the easter egg on the copper highlight of the front passenger seat:

What a beauty. TATA design team is on a roll for few years now !! This car is pretty wide and i would say compete with the likes of Venue and Sonet (dimension wise).

I wonder how they will position this(or moving nexon towards EV space). Or perhaps a Jimny(not sure if that has market in the first place) competition ?

Very good design. Modern and sturdy. Will it sit above or below Nexon?

Tata's HBX/H2X resumes its testing:






LInk

More images: Tata H2X sub-4 meter SUV

The Tata H2X sub-4 meter SUV has been spotted testing with what appear to be production-spec tail lamp clusters.



Tata's upcoming sub-4 meter SUV is based on the HBX concept that was showcased at the 2020 Auto Expo. The SUV features Tata's Impact 2.0 design language. The test car has oval tail lamps with Y-shaped brake lights. The tail lamp clusters house diamond shaped turn indicators as well.

The H2X test mule has a high-mounted stop lamp positioned above the rear windshield. It also appears to have reflectors on the rear bumpers.



The H2X was supposed to be launched in the second half of this year. However, reports suggest that the launch might have been pushed to FY2021.

Quote:
Originally Posted by warrioraks 
Noob question - how does one infer the production readiness from camouflaged spy shots? I have seen similar posts on other threads as well but can’t seem to understand what’s the trick here.
According to me, a production ready vehicle has everything in place, this means that the car would have the actual DRLs, LEDs and everything in place just like any car is supposed to look. A car that is going through testing would have some equipment attached and you won’t see diamond cut alloys on a vehicle that is going through testing, just basic steel wheels. They don’t put all the gizmos on a test vehicle.

Just to give you an example, the picture here is of a production ready vehicle. But the spy shots that are surfacing of XUV500 for example, are of a vehicle that is going through testing, well that’s my understanding.

Hope it helps!

The HBX is based on the same platform as the Altroz. It is carrying the same engines, same premium features, such as the TFT instrument cluster, cruise control, etc.

It is not going to be a beefed-up budget hatchback and will be a proper crossover.

Power figures of the HBX NA petrol and Turbo Petrol (if launched) are similar.

It is also safe to assume that the HBX will be slightly more premium (touch & feel, safety) than the Magnite.

If I am in the market for a sub 4 metre crossover, apart from Magnite, I will take a look at both Nexon and HBX.

As it is already stated in every T-BHP thread that design is a subjective thing, I understand people liking or disliking the design. But the more interesting part to note here is the wizardry manufacturers do in Auto Expo showcases of their cars:

Multiple overhead lights pointing down on a highly glossy and polished car with a bright body colour decidedly makes the cuts, creases and overall design more prominent and beautiful; but when we see it in photographs taken under direct sunlight everyone can’t help but compare it to those shiny shots from the expo.
Manufacturers darken the headlights and tail-lights giving them a more modern smoky appearance; and tint window panes of the showcase cars to compliment the floating roof design. Those cars aren’t road legal anyway, so this definitely increases the appeal.
Camouflaged cars decidedly look very ugly. The recent spy shots of the rear end of upcoming XUV500 looks as if some kid designed it as a stick figure, but we are yet to see the finished product and how the lines and creases come together so there’s that.
Funky alloys and “thiccc” chunky tyres. It is evident Tata and every other manufacturer goes gaga over their alloy designs in concept cars. The HBX had very prominent wheel arches showcasing very beautiful yet impractical alloys shod with very thick and almost off-road tyres. But this photo shows it sporting bland steel wheels with puny rubbers.
Lastly, when we follow the threads or news on an upcoming car for quite some time it loses the novelty factor for us and we start feeling as if there is nothing new in this one; that is, until we see it in person for the first time and the same feeling comes back.

Typical of Tata - the production spec resembles concept quite a lot. Good to see the trend continue.

On the other hand - it was while looking at this picture that I realized for the first time - the front windscreen is almost F-L-A-T. Very minimal curvature to it. Interesting to see how it works out on the aerodynamics and wind noise management. Also - flatter glass is considered to be cheaper than a curved one. I don't know if its true of course - but that's what I have always heard. There is a minimal/no sun-shade on the top edge at all. Just a patch at the center to avoid glare on the head unit screen. It was the same way on the concept too. Just didn't realize it till now. Goes to show how attention grabbing the rest of the concept design is/was.

Scoop! Tata HBX 5-speed MT interior spied

New spy images of the Tata HBX have revealed the interiors of the sub-4 meter crossover. The car seen in these images is the 5-speed manual variant.



The dashboard layout looks similar to that of the HBX Concept. The centre console houses a floating touchscreen infotainment head-unit. The 3-spoke flat-bottom steering wheel is also identical to the one seen on the concept car.



The spy images reveal the HBX's rather smart looking dual-tone alloy wheels and the unique teardrop shaped wraparound tail lamps. We also know that the front fascia of the HBX is heavily inspired by the Harrier. It has a slim grille flanked by LED daytime running lights and headlamps positioned lower down.



The Tata HBX is likely to be offered with a 1.2-litre petrol engine producing 85 BHP and 113 Nm, while higher variants might get a turbocharged 1.2-litre motor. Transmission options might include a 5-speed manual and 5-speed AMT.

Thanks to Prajwal Narayan for sending these images in. Heartfelt gratitude for sharing them with other enthusiasts via this Team-BHP page!

Tata has always said that unlike its multinational competitors, it doesn't have the luxury of scale.

Whereas the likes of Koreans and Europeans can choose an existing engine or transmission from their international portfolio and afford to present all at the time of launch, Tata has to take time to either develop or outsource while considering whether there will be enough scale to devote time and resources.

But, with the HBX, they finally have some advantage. The Altroz, based on the same platform, is already on the road.

It has an NA petrol, a Turbo petrol and a Diesel. Apart from a 5 speed manual transmission, a DCT is also in the works.

I would really hope Tata goes all guns blazing with the HBX.

Even if the base variants have an NA petrol, transmissions both manual and AMT, they should really consider adding in the Turbo Petrol and DCT even if at a later stage.

The new i20 is a good example that people are willing to pay extra for extra features.

One, a 30k premium over Altroz, considering traditionally crossovers are more expensive than their hatch siblings.
Two, a 40-50k premium for AMT over manual. Took this from the Tigor pricing.
Three, a 60k premium for TC over NA. Took this from the Altroz pricing.
Four, a 1 lakh premium for DCT over manual. A wild guess here.

More images: Tata HBX spied; front fascia & dual-tone alloys

A test mule of the upcoming Tata HBX has been spotted in Trivandrum, Kerala.



While the test car is still under heavy camouflage, there are a few details worth mentioning. The HBX's styling is based on Tata's Impact 2.0 design philosophy. It features a piano black front fascia and a familiar Y-pattern grille. The car has LED daytime running lights with the main headlamp clusters positioned lower down.



At the rear, it gets hexagonal tail lamp clusters with Y-shaped brake lights. This particular test car is also equipped with fog lamps and dual-tone alloy wheels.



On the interior, the HBX gets a floating touchscreen infotainment head unit. Lower variants of the car will get a smaller screen.



The Tata HBX is likely to be offered with a 1.2-litre petrol engine producing 85 BHP and 113 Nm, while higher variants might get a turbocharged 1.2-litre motor. Transmission options might include a 5-speed manual and 5-speed AMT.

Thanks to Jithin for sending these images in. Heartfelt gratitude for sharing them with other enthusiasts via this Team-BHP page!

I think it is high time that Tata junks its clunky AMT, or at least limit it to the Tiago. It is too jerky and unrefined for a modern automobile.

While the upcoming DCT may be too expensive for this application, they seriously need to consider developing Hyundai's iMT style automated clutch type solutions. All the convenience without the added complexity or cost. Evidently, iMT has been very well received by the market. Tata should pursue this seriously and quickly.

Production-spec Tata HBX crossover spotted

New images of the production-spec Tata HBX have surfaced online.



The Tata HBX is based on the ALFA platform which is shared with the Altroz. The exterior design of the sub-4 meter crossover is based on Tata's Impact 2.0 design philosophy. It gets a Harrier-like front fascia with slim LED daytime running lights on either side of the grille and headlamps positioned lower down on the front bumper.

As you can see in these images, the HBX features a rugged look courtesy of the black body cladding. One of the cars has a dual-tone exterior finished in blue and white. It appears to be a higher variant as it is equipped with fog lamps and dual-tone alloy wheels.



The Tata HBX is likely to be offered with a 1.2-litre petrol engine producing 85 BHP and 113 Nm, while higher variants might get a turbocharged 1.2-litre motor. Transmission options might include a 5-speed manual and 5-speed AMT.

Source: Motorbeam

Quote:

I find it a little amusing that nowadays we call the tallboy hatches as micro-SUV
SUV is more of a generic term just like CAR. From a Tata Nano to a Lamborghini Urus, everything is classified as a CAR. SUV obviously is a sub-set in the CAR category. In India, we have multiple iterations of an SUV. We have the "proper" SUVs, compact, sub-compact, mini and micro SUVs. Some day we might even have a "nano" SUV, who knows! The terms Pseudo SUV and Crossover are also widely used. With no strict definition, the term SUV is generally used to describe vehicles based on their body style, without attaching much significance to the vehicle's on-road/off-road capabilities. Attaching Wikipedia link for reference.

https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sport_utility_vehicle

Scoop! Tata Hornbill (HBX) production kicks-off

Tata Motors seems to be gearing up to launch the Hornbill (HBX) as we have learned that production has already started and the first batch of cars is ready.



The Tata HBX is based on the ALFA platform which is shared with the Altroz. The crossover is likely to be offered with a 1.2-litre petrol engine producing 85 BHP and 113 Nm. Higher variants of the car might get a turbocharged 1.2-litre motor, while transmission options are likely to include a 5-speed manual and 5-speed AMT.

The production version looks very similar to the HBX Concept that was showcased at the 2020 Auto Expo. The exterior styling is based on Tata's Impact 2.0 philosophy. The car has a Harrier-like front fascia with slim LED daytime running lights on either side of the grille and headlamps positioned lower down on the front bumper.

The Tata HBX will compete with the Maruti Suzuki Ignis and the upcoming Hyundai Casper (AX1).

Thanks to the Team-BHP fan (he prefers to remain anonymous) who sent this information in. Heartfelt gratitude for sharing it with other enthusiasts via this Team-BHP share page!

Interesting name. Since it's just the name that has been revealed I'll limit my thoughts with the same.

So what does "Punch" stand here for?

Is it the elusive Indian name for which we have created a dedicated thread?

Wait! What?

Yup according to Merriam-webster


Quote:
Punch, referring to a beverage, indicates either a spiced concoction of hard liquor, wine, or beer and fruit juices (sometimes tea), or a refreshing, nonalcoholic mixture of fruit juices often with carbonated soda added; both are made in "party" size quantities. This punch is first served in early 17th-century English, and it is believed to have originated from Hindi and Urdu pā̃c, meaning "five"—in reference to the supposed original number of ingredients, which probably consisted of an alcohol (such as arrack), a sour (such as lemon or lime), a sugar or other sweetener, spices (or tea), and water. Inevitably, there were variations in recipes, as there are today.

The Hindi/Urdu word derives from Sanskrit pañca, which is an ingredient in the word pañcāmṛta, meaning, literally, "five nectars (of the gods)" and refers to a mixture of five ingredients—in particular, milk, curd, butter (or ghee), honey, and sugar (or molasses)—believed to have medicinal properties when combined. Another related Sanskrit word is pañcapātra, meaning "set of five glass bowls for libations."
Further more;


Quote:
Punch having meanings indicating "hitting," "pushing/pressing down," or "making holes in" is believed to be etymologically related to puncheon, as well as pounce. This punch has hit English with quite a few words and phrases. For example, there's punch holes in (to weaken), punch line (something said that makes the point), punch up (to give forcefulness to), beat someone to the punch (to do something before someone else; to land your punch first), pack a punch (to be strong or forceful), pull punches (to use less force), and roll with the punches (to adjust to what is coming; to move so as to lessen the blow).

Tata has perfected the reuse of parts across platforms - Infotainment, Steering wheel and switches, Auto HVAC, Instrument cluster etc... And this is good for both customers, OE and the service guys as the logistics, inventory maintenance and cost of design and production/procurement (read volume negotiations) are reduced.

To explain the complexity behind this for some, it's easy to standardize non functional or non customer touchpoint parts. But to make the HMI, displays and switches common comes with a lot of forethought. This is because there are differences in electrical architecture (hardware and Software). Older generation cars from domestic OEs didn't focus much on common architecture, but now they are mature. Then comes the design (styling) part, where you need to reflect the character of the car/segment and still have reduced part variations. For example, Sport cars feature a different HMI and lighting theme and color accents than a elegant business vehicle. And not to mention, packaging the same hardware in different sizes of space available.

My 2 points about the look of the car:
- The interiors look neat, I like their is not a lot going on there. The terrain modes or drive modes seem to be missing( no knobs or buttons) as seen in the latest teaser.

- The boot space looks like it's from XUV 300. Around 250ltrs which is small if a family is planning a long trip.
- shark fin antenna and rear AC vents are missing even from a top model.

Nothing yet to be a deal breaker but these things reassure that they have tried to cut cost wherever possible which is a good sign for someone like me who doesn't have a big budget and needs a basic car with minimum requirements so the car might be starting with a very attractive price at launch.

Tata Punch to be unveiled on October 4, 2021

The Tata Punch will be officially unveiled October 4, 2021.



The Punch is based on the ALFA-ARC (Agile Light Flexible Advanced Architecture) platform and features Tata's Impact 2.0 design.

The crossover gets a Harrier-like front fascia with a split grille. It gets slim LED daytime running lights, arrow-shaped tail lamps and a chunky dual-tone bumper. Higher variants of the car will get dual-tone colour options and diamond-cut alloy wheels.

The crossover comes with a dual-tone interior, with colour-matched inserts around the air-conditioner vents. A floating touchscreen infotainment system sits at the top of the centre console. The car is equipped with automatic climate control, cruise control and a semi-digital instrument cluster similar to that of the Harrier.

The Tata Punch is likely to be offered with a 1.2-litre petrol engine producing 85 BHP and 113 Nm. Higher variants of the car might get a turbocharged 1.2-litre motor, while transmission options are likely to include a 5-speed manual and 5-speed AMT.

Quote:

I'm confused now, Punch is going toe to toe with Nexon. I'm unable to make sense of what the Nexon is offering more than Punch. Are the internal parts of lower quality? Will the cost of spares offset the less price of the car?
Nexon is still a bigger car than the Punch in every aspect. Punch will have a GC of around 180mm while the Nexon has a GC of 209mm. I expect the rear seat of the Punch to be less comfortable and spacious than the Nexon.

The big differentiator is likely to be the engine performance. The Punch won't get a diesel engine like the Nexon and the turbo-petrol is still not confirmed as per the leaks. Even if it gets the turbo-petrol, it is likely to get the Altroz's state of tune with the 5 speed MT.

I don't know what Tata is planning with the Nexon but it would be due for an upgrade in a couple years' time and Tata may move the Nexon to a slightly higher segment to try and plug the gap between the present gen Nexon and Harrier. With the Blackbird concept scrapped, this may be the strategy that Tata may adopt to take on the Creta and Seltos.

Currently we are spoilt for choice in the sub-4m car market. The ludicrous 4m rule has ensured that there are enough number of cars just about nudging the mark. Also, there is no dearth of choice in the small car segment(3600 to 3750mm length). In the midst of this, it is interesting to note that there are actually not many cars filling the void between the aforementioned sub segments. Currently I see only a very few cars bridging the void, mainly the Swift(3845mm) and the Grand i10 Nios(3805mm). With Tata Punch measuring about 3840mm(perceived), it's not all that hard to imagine where Tata is aiming to land the PUNCH.

I have said this before, saying it again- if priced well, Punch has all the right ingredients to be Tata's best seller.

IMHO Tata Punch has success written all over it.


Looks: Looks matter a lot. I feel it is better than Nexon for two reasons 1)The front fascia including the headlights resemble the Harrier, which in comparison to Nexon where it looks bit dated 2) The window line which rises upward in Nexon is radical but a flatter one is better for back seat users.


Dimensions, interiors: It is the widest in the B2 segment at 1800mm in comparison to Ignis(1690), Swift(1735), Grand i10(1680). In fact it is wider than Kiger/Magnite(1750-1760). In one of the youtube videos taken down, the central tunnel appeared to be very minimal and the back seat was good enough for 3 passengers when compared to many rivals. Of course, the incline angle was more upright.


SUVish stance: The Harrier front looks, width of car, proportional looks, 187mm GC, and the overall muscular shape is in line with the current mass market demand for butch looking cars. I found the ingress-egress in Nexon rear door difficult due to the 210 mm GC and the door sill which isn't wide. The Punch should be better in this area.


Safety: Being built on ALFA architecture it can score at least 4 star NCAP rating, making it safer than the likes of Swift, i10 and in the league of Tigor, Tiago.


Pricing: The pricing is expected to be in the Kiger 1.0 NA engine range of 5.5 to 8.5 L, including the AMT options. Consider that the 1.2L Revotron (86PS, 113 Nm), is powerful than the 1.0 of Kiger.

With these I believe Punch should be successful than Kiger-Magnite, Tiago, Ignis and also eat into the share of lower variants in Nexon, Venue-Sonet.

Yes, the engine is gruffy, but Tiago/Tigor sell well with the same engines.
Media Drives start next week. Hopefully should be ready to launch.

Tata Punch officially revealed



Tata Punch is based on the ALFA architecture, same as the Altroz, but adapted for an SUV.



The pillars on which the Punch stands on.



Tata Punch will be powered by a 1.2-litre petrol engine that notable features automatic engine/start stop functionality to aid fuel efficiency.



Tata Punch notably features a high altitude map for the AMT box's software and a traction mode to aid handling in slippery conditions.





Tata Punch also features a segment-first Brake Sway Control feature that helps with stability under hard braking.



The ground clearance on the Punch is rated at 187 mm.



The approach, departure and ramp over angles on the Punch.



The Punch comes with a luggage capacity of 366 litres.



The Punch comes with 90 degree opening doors for easy ingress and Egress.



Dual airbags are standard on the Punch and also get ISOFIX child seat safety mounts.

Event update:
Pricing hasn't been announced. All the trims look good and loaded.

Tata Punch variants details

The Punch will come with dual tone colour options



The Punch will be available with various Personas, this is the base variant of the SUV.



The Pure Persona variant of the Punch is available in 2 colours



There is the Adventure Persona which comes with features like a 4-inch Harman infotainment system, steering mounted controls and more.



The Accomplished Persona which gets a host of different features.



The top-spec variant is called Creative Persona and it's the one with the connectivity features. The Punch is a connected car.



And here are what's available across the variants.

This vehicle is most probably for those who can afford an Altroz, but want an SUV-ish vehicle with higher ground clearance.

This will be on the list for the kind of buyers who has fixed their budget, but don't know what car they want. For someone like that, this ticks almost all the boxes.

I couldn't help but have a gnawing thought at the back of my mind, that shouldn't Tata Motors have issued a disclaimer / warning that the event that they had organised was performed by a highly experienced driver who has cut her teeth, literally, driving on that route ever since the time she obtained her driving licence?

As the PUNCH will be a mass market offering and relatively affordable to a large section of the car buying populace, I fervently hope, tourists with 2WD vehicles and
future PUNCH owners, exercise the utmost of caution and not do anything foolhardy to put their and other's lives at risk.

There may be howls of disdain and a lot of skeptics may say that mountains are being made out of molehills about the difficulty index of the route; but having driven that route a 4 times, albeit in the "non Facebook, YouTube, Instagram" era, in a carburettered Gypsy, the route should not be scoffed at by youngsters of today.

It's not only about gradient and altitude, it's about finicky weather systems, occasional slush, ice, slippery boulders with zero traction surfaces which you have to have a knack for spotting (and trying) to avoid a wheel running over it. It's about modulating throttle inputs, placing your wheels on surfaces where the textbook would not want them to be placed.
You have to be one with your car, sniff the clutch to understand when to ease up, sensitise your backside to underbody hits and ensure the "hits" are in "safe zones" and at the same time maintain momentum. It's not only the performance envelope of your car; but also your skills, reflexes and strong nerves.
Ask people like our very own D-Bhp-ian's Samba & Blackpearl, who have plied this route a million times and they'll tell you.

Also Sandakphu is not merely a 4x4 trail as to whose car does better.
It is a very spiritual journey. The locals revere and venerate the mountains, the chortens, it's holy places and lakes.
Hope we do not end up having hordes of "punchy" folks attempting to traipse their way up & destroy the sanctity of the surroundings knowingly or unknowingly.

Sorry for the long-ish and maybe a bit of a dampener of a post. It's just that these thoughts were scurrying around in my mind and thought of sharing with all of you.

Drive safe,

Best....,

Although 0-100 in 16.xx seconds cannot be classified as nimble, it is no slouch either for a kind of car Punch is. This is just one of the many parameters of a vehicle. I am of the belief that Tata have probably geared the Punch for better low end grunt than outright acceleration. I might be completely wrong here, but the promotional videos at-least suggest so. We generally tend to go only by the numbers with regards to on-road performance- bhp/ps rating, torque rating, number of cylinders and for that matter even the number of gears. Quite a few of us(including me), on occasions fail to realise that gearing has a major say in a vehicle's performance. Just to clarify my viewpoint, consider good old Mahindra UVs. Power ratings, acceleration figures may not be hair raising. Forget 4 speed, even with a 3 speed gearbox, low end grunt the UVs possess is immense and the fun to drive character is beyond words. I know, Punch can't be compared to the rugged UVs. I am only talking from the gearing perspective. I am also pretty sure, folks who have ridden Hero Honda CD100 on broken rural roads will not disagree.

I'm happy Tata is bringing affordable safety to more segments but I really wonder how they intend to score well without Electronic Stability Control and side head protection airbags which are requirements for higher Global NCAP stars starting next year.
For example, for the year 2022 (it becomes more stringent for later years):
Besides qualifying for the minimum required scores for each star in the front and side impact, and meeting some seatbelt reminder requirements, the following requirements have to be met including GTR9-compliant pedestrian protection:
3 stars: ESC optional on at least one variant
4 stars: GTR8 compliant ESC meeting certain fitment rates, side head protection optional on at least one variant with performance verified by in-house test data
5 stars: GTR8 compliant ESC meeting certain fitment rates identical to that for four stars, side head protection fit on at least 30% of variants sold with performance verified by a lateral pole test sponsored with Global NCAP
The Punch does not meet any of these and I wonder what Tata would do if Global NCAP decides to assess/reassess the Punch next year. The masses would not take kindly to a two-star Tata, even if the protocol is different (something many will very likely fail to understand).

What is the point of mentioning Performance Figures, when they are this poor?
Even vehicles which have sub 11s timings are shying away from these kind of claims.
Also, rather than outright performance, roll-on figures i.e. drivability numbers make more sense.

Nevertheless, this is a charming design, and is arguably much more butch in appearance that it's competitors.
We can safely assume that this will a great performer at the sales chart, just like it's elder cousin Nexon.

Tata Punch production plans:

1. Plans to produce 8,000 Punch cars (or Punches) in October.
2. Intends to manufacture 35,000 to 37,000 cars in total in October, highest in Tata's history
3.Punch's production will necessitate the start of a 3rd shift at the Pune plant, something that has not happened in a decade.
4. Punch will allow the Tata domestic PV business to breakeven.
5. Tata aims for a regular 35,000 sales per month from now onwards.

A lot has been said about the engine.

My Tigor has the same engine.

Yes, it can reach 100 kph.

Yes, it is faster than a truck. Can overtake one easily.

It ain't no enthusiast's favourite, but does the job and gives me 16+ kpl easily.

Plus, I have the AMT too. I stopped getting gear shift jerks the day I got used to it. That was a couple of days. Now, my passengers can't even notice when the gear changes.

The engine ain't fast but it ain't that slow either, the way it is being made out to be.

Not all want to push the pedal to the metal. Not all are in race mode all the time. Not all want to reach triple digit speeds the moment the light turns green.

Its good for sedate driving and suitable for a large majority of people. Something the sales figures reflect too.

Many fellow enthusiasts here are defending the weak engine of TATA Punch but while i do understand that not everyone wants a fast car but its not always about speed. A strong engine helps you overtake easily on those narrow 2 lane roads. It makes driving in ghats easy when the car is loaded. It helps in cutting down travel time for long distances. So i dont know why one can not complain of such a weak engine without being labelled as a pedal to the metal driver.

Another noteworthy feature of Punch is its water wading capacity of 370mm which is far better than that of Nexon(< 300mm) and cars couple of segments above. AFAIK, Ecosport is the only car which is having better water wading capacity(550mm) in <14L bracket. This will be boon considering the surprises we expect in monsoons.

Ghat driving, 2 lane roads etc mostly you will be in the 30-70 speed range, which is taken care of by gearing rather than outright power. It’s the availability of ‘torque’ at almost flat rating over a range of 700-1000 rpm that will give you the ‘pull’ to overtake.
For all the talk about power to overtake and ghar driving - the car of choice in the hills is the humble Alto/erstwhile 800/ old Santro
All cars with nimble handling, strong torque delivery in lower gears (travel to Himachal, Sikkim, Arunachal ) and you will know what I am talking about.

Not defending Tata here - far from
It. They should have put the Turbo Petrol or a NA Diesel for sure - but I think for the common public who spend 99% of their driving time in cities 0-60 matters more than 0-100 times.

Without knowing the gear ratios (and some more stuff), it's futile to comment on the power and torque of a vehicle. Surely, these are important parameters along with Power-to-weight and Torque-to-weight ratios. However, between the engine and wheels, there is transmission.

An OEM selects gear ratios based on various boundary conditions, including drivability/ convenience for target customers. (there may still be unsatisfied customers, but then you can't please all for a given sale price).

Most customers take only few days to adapt to drivability of new vehicle and absolute numbers (engine output at crank) are soon forgotten.

I checked out the Punch today at a Tata dealership. I am scouring for a car for my Brother In Law whose current ride is a Mahindra KUV 100. Punch is a strong contender so my comparison is with KUV 100 since he wants to replace it with a new car.

The Car is built Tight. Front of the Car looks Muscular. You will easily take it to be a Nexon on a casual glance. Its that Muscular. For an untrained eye Nexon and Punch side by side will look same cars.
Contrasting colours brings out the Muscular aspects of the car. Dark colours makes the car look like (if I may say this) beefed up S-Presso.
Car is spacious by the segment standards. Definitely not Nexon/KUV 100 Space. High headroom will be highly useful. Flat Floor and adequate space between rear Blower Vents and Seat base. So middle passenger will feel welcome. Some angles shows the Car as a mix of multiple cars.
Rear Silhouette reminded me of S-Presso albeit a beefed up one.
The Boot Door is small and the size with respect to the Body is Kwid...sh. The boot door reminded me of Kwid.
Ground Clearance is very good. People will do well to scrape its underbody. SA told me the Top End Manual is expected around 8.5 Lakhs. Not sure if this is possible. No idea about price variation of Manual Vs AMT.
Front Doors felt not so heavy (Ref. Nexon). Rear Doors felt Light. Boot Door did not have any weight, felt light (I am comparing against Nexon and KUV 100). But the sound upon closing the doors is good. Does not sound Flimsy. Boot Door sounds Flimsy.

Mod Note: Our Tata Punch Review has been taken live at this link. Please continue the discussion over on the new thread. Thanks!

Hello Folks,

I’m choosing a base automatic hatchback for primarily city use with an on-road budget of less than ₹10 lakh.

Finalist cars are:

Hyundai i20 Magna IVT – ₹9.75 lakh (on-road)

Tata Altroz Pure Petrol 5AMT – ₹9.15 lakh (on-road)

I’m looking for real-world impressions from owners and test-drivers of either / both.

Specifically, I’d like to know which one you feel is better in terms of:

1. Engine refinement & responsiveness in the city

2. Gearbox behaviour in stop-go traffic (IVT vs AMT in actual use)

3. Overall city comfort & NVH

For someone who will be using the car almost entirely in the city, which of these two would you pick, and why?

Thanks in advance for your inputs.

1. As regards engine refinement and responsiveness, the Hyundai i20 IVT is clearly the better pick. The NVH levels are lower in the i20 as compared to the Altroz.

2. The i20 IVT wins on gearbox behaviour in stop-and-go traffic with smoother power delivery as compared to the head-nods on the AMT in the Altroz.

3. The overall city comfort and NVH are better on the i20 as well.

Another alternative for better mileage and refinement is the Maruti Baleno AGS, which feels better than the Altroz on that front, but not up to the level of the i20 IVT

This is a no-brainer, really. I'd choose the i20 all day, everyday.

I haven't driven the variants you mentioned specifically, but I did drive the pre-facelift Altroz 1.2 MT, Tiago 1.2L AMT and the Hyundai 1.2L engine in other cars.

1. Nothing beats Hyundai engines when it comes to sheer smoothness and refinement. They may not be the peppiest among the 1.2L engines of the competition, but they trump everyone on the refinement front. The Tata 1.2L engine on the other hand is very unrefined, and it just about okay when it comes to performance in the city. Even in the manual version, I had to work the gearbox to extract meaningful performance out of that engine.

2. It is a well known fact that Tata, Renault and Mahindra make some of the worst AMTs around. Head nods are inescapable in the AMT, and they dull the driving experience – refinement and performance both. On the other hand, CVTs in general are far smoother and superior to drive in the city (I'll leave owners to talk about the i20's CVT since I haven't driven one myself).

3. I found the suspension setup of the Altroz to be stiff and better suited for highways. The i20 should be more comfortable for city drives. In terms of NVH, again, the i20 trumps the Altroz. Engine noise is too high in the latter. Can't comment on sound insulation, road/wind/tyre noise, seat cushioning in either car.


The extra 60k that the i20 Magna commands is worth it in the long term, even if it has lesser features than the Altroz Pure. You'll enjoy driving it in the city. Not to mention, Hyundai ASS > Tata ASS. The Altroz's high safety score would be relevant only if you were to use the car on highways extensively. This would've been an advantage in the Altroz's favour but given your use case, don't worry about it at all.

Hope this helps. Happy shopping!

PS: Any reason to not consider the Baleno?

Honestly the Honda Amaze and Baleno/Fronx come with better drivetrains but if you have to choose between the Altroz and the i20 then clearly features and styling matter more to you. Both are refined more so due to added damping rather than inherently good engine harmonics. The i20 has the superior gearbox of the two.

Between the two, the i20 Magna IVT is the better city car by a clear margin. It’s smoother, quieter and far more refined in traffic. The IVT gearbox makes a big difference—no jerks, no hesitation, just clean and easy progress. The Altroz is a solid car, but the AMT simply can’t match the i20’s calmness and polish in stop-go conditions.
If you’re open to looking beyond these two, also check out the Honda Amaze CVT. For pure city use, it’s one of the smoothest and most effortless automatics you can buy in this budget.

Voted for Altroz AMT as I bought one after test driving both the cars.

So here is my comparison of various factors.

NVH is no doubt better in I20, although Altroz facelift has improved NVH than the outgoing model, yet doesn't come close to I20.

Peppyness of engine is better in altroz, I 20 has a very mellowed down engine and the CVT makes it a bit more rubbery.

Bumper to bumper traffic altroz will give a slightly better FE of between 12-9 kmpl, in the I20 IVT expect that to be between 8-9 kmpl.

Interior and exterior, Altroz feels a bit more premium and modern, I20 is almost 5 years old, without a substantial facelift so is looking dated, that gray shade on interior is not to everyone's liking.

Feature wise Altroz Creative S AMT(top most model in AMT) is on par with I20 Sportz O IVT model

Suspension and ride quality of Altroz is a wee bit better than i20, say the difference is 20:18.

Service experience will be better with i20, I have my first free service schedule and already there was some good up from the service center on the booking date, this has already started giving me butterflies in my stomach.

Safety- altroz scores 5 stars, I20 scores 3

Chose altroz as I20 was really looking dated and don't like the design(personal preference)

As an ex-owner of i20 IVT 2021(same generation as currently running, pre facelift), can definitely tell you it will be better than any AMT car on sale today, the money you save in an AMT will definitely be lesser than the drive experience an IVT will incrementally offer.
Also i20 interior quality is a notch above with better ergonomics and a smoother power train because of the 4 cylinder engine, yes it is not crazy fast, but will still be faster than the altroz.
I really like how the altroz pre facelift looked, cannot say same for the current one on sale, but it's a subjective topic.
Hyundai will also offer good deals as the car in question is a high selling model and someone at some dealership might have handy ready stock.

Cheers

Voted for the Tata Altroz

Last year, I would have said to pick the Hyundai i20, as the Tata Altroz was looking dated and there was no news of an update. But now we have the new Tata Altroz, which in my opinion offers a fresher look compared with the Hyundai i20. It has been a few years since the i20 received its last update, and it’s starting to look a bit old. If you prefer a relaxed driving style, the AMT shouldn’t be much of an issue for you. Additionally, Hyundai cars are not known for their fuel efficiency, and you are likely to get better fuel economy with the Tata Altroz.

There are still a few points, such as after-sales service and NVH, where you may find the Hyundai i20 to be better.

I would also suggest considering the Honda Amaze. You might get a good offer, and it’s a nice city car as well.

Best wishes!

While the AMTs have improved over the years, and in the last decade, we have seen lot of models with AMT's, they still are not as good as torque converters or CVTs. My brother in law has a i20 Sportz IVT and we have a Grand i10 Nios AMT(These cars have the same engine,but different gearboxes). While the Grand i10 Nios AMT is one of the best avaiable, it still gets jerky in early gears and gets confused when you give sudden accelerator inputs. The clutch still exists in AMT which will see wear and tear over the years, and the experience diminises with clutch wear. The IVT has the rubber band effect and there is a slight lag in the normal mode, the throttle control gets better in the sports mode. Even in terms of fuel efficiency I dont see much of a difference between the i20 and the Grand i10 in city usage.

Having been a Tata car owner previously (we had a Nano XTA before the Grand i10) and suffering thorugh their service over multiple centers across Bangalore, I would recommend the i20 over the Altroz anytime.

I drive a Tata Punch AMT, which has the same engine / gearbox as the Altroz. The engine is definitely not Punchy, but is quite alright once you get used to its power delivery. I feel the AMT gearbox is definitely poor. It makes the car really sluggish whether it is driven enthusiastically or in a sedate manner.
The engine is not well refined either. NVH levels are ok, since I haven't driven the i20 iVT, I am unable to compare. I am getting 10 kmpl in the city (My driving is not sedate or relaxed). When I drive in a relaxed manner, it gives me about 12.50 kmpl.

Wishing you all the best!
Purely for the CVT gearbox, I will suggest i20.

Haven't driven either but if going with i20 , do consider top or mid variant. The base magna variant is too boring. My brother owned the sportz version manual and it was loaded.
Do note that the 1.2 kappa engine feels dead in mid rev range so its not much fun either. Cvts need linear, high revving engines like Honda's.

Honda has developed their cvts in house for longest time, while Hyundai sources it from a relatively new inhouse supplier (Transys?) so not as perfectly mated as Toyota/Honda.

Altroz looks fresh and whatever drawbacks it has in the powertrain department, it makes up with all goodies like good music system, sunroof etc. Not to mention a good European handling.

I'd suggest the i20 IVT any day over Tata's AMT. Someone I know had bought a Tiago AMT in 2022 by replacing his 2020 Polo 1.0 MPI MT, but had to sell it off within a year due to poor pickup and the lethargic gearbox, as well as poor FE.

He again bought a car with an AMT gearbox, which is way smoother than the Tata AMT gearbox and fuel efficient, i.e, Toyota Glanza.

Or else, stretch your budget and get Amaze CVT.

If AMT, I won't recommend getting a Tata vehicle. I'd be better off with a Maruti or Hyundai.

Also, i20's CVT gearbox is way smoother in its segment.

I thinks that the IVTs are similar to CVT gear box, while an AMT used in TATA Altros merely is an Automated Manual transmission. While AMT may offer better fuel efficiency on paper, in my experience, CVT gives better mileage in real time. The TATA Tiago AMT I used to own used to hold onto lower gears more, on a patchy road, thus giving lower fuel efficiency than my CVT cars.

I always felt that the gear shifts in AMT was more noticeable while the CVT provides a seamless experience through the ratios.

Having driven the cvts and amt sub 12 lakh 1 and 1.2 lt NA cars, I have a somewhat contrary opinion. Given the limited torque produced by these engines, I feel that fixed ratio gearboxes are best suited. With a CVT and its various nomenclature variants, the ratio hunt and rubber band effect is just too damn annoying. They are enjoyable if and only if you are a sedate driver and drive primarily on flat elevations. And the thing is, if you drive a current gen AMT in a similarly relaxed manner, they are fairly smooth as well.

For me, I would anyday prefer the predictable throttle input-> rpm rise->actual speed gain of an amt, its better fuel efficiency, lack of headache wrt gearbox oil changes and lower maintenance cost, cheaper component replacement costs to a cvt on a 1.2 NA engine. The modern AMT have a nice hill hold as well, eliminating one of the major practical issues with some of the earlier implementations. Plus the manual triptronic like mode is smooth enough and quick enough for most of the usage these 1.2 NA cars will go through.

Its quite easy to learn to either modulate and lift off the throttle while driving hard for a smoother shift or to just adopt a relaxed driving pattern. You can use the manual mode for quick overtakes/hill driving. But there is absolutely no workaround to the horrible rubberband effect if you want to get a move on in a cvt. A little bit of power delay between shifts on an amt over the excessive engine noise of the cvts for me anyday.

Anything more than a 1.2NA and I would whole heartedly go with a CVT. But for me they just dont work with a small NA or a small turbo charged engine.

edit- would prefer a maruti/toyota or hyundai amt over the tata and mahindra though

2026 Tata Punch Facelift Review

This review has been jointly compiled with Omkar. Thanks to him for the expert observations and photography!


Since the Tata Punch has already been reviewed by Team-BHP, this report will focus solely on the changes in the facelift model.

Tata Punch Review

Introduction

Back in 2021, when Tata first launched the Punch, it was among the first few crossovers, alongside the Renault Kiger and Nissan Magnite, to compete in a segment below cars like the Nexon, Maruti Suzuki Brezza and Hyundai Venue. The timing was perfect, and the sales started to come in for the Punch. To put things into perspective, Tata has sold over 7 lakh units of the Punch in just four years. In fact, it went on to become the country’s highest-selling car in 2024.

The Punch’s popularity can be attributed to its mini-SUV styling, high ground clearance, sorted suspension setup, a decent cabin experience and impressive safety credentials (the pre-facelift model scored 5 stars in the GNCAP crash tests). Tata has also been proactive with timely updates, which helped the Punch retain its competitive edge against newer rivals such as the Hyundai Exter, Maruti Suzuki Fronx/Toyota Taisor and Citroen C3.

If there was one chink in the Punch’s armour, it was its 1.2L NA three-cylinder engine. While it delivered adequate performance for city usage, highway performance often left a lot of people wanting more. With the 2026 Punch facelift, Tata has addressed this issue to a large extent by introducing its 118 BHP 1.2L turbo-petrol engine, borrowed from the Nexon. That said, this engine is currently available only with a 6-speed manual gearbox.

On the other hand, Tata has also extended its CNG + AMT combination from Tiago & Tigor to the Punch. Along with these mechanical updates, the facelift also brings a host of changes to the exterior and interior styling, as well as additional features. The 1.2-litre NA engine is offered with both manual and AMT gearbox options. Read on to find out how the new powertrain options feel to drive in the real world and whether the updates are enough to strengthen the Punch’s case further.


Tata Punch Facelift Price & Brochure

The Punch facelift is priced at Rs 5.59 lakh (ex-showroom) onwards. For reference, the facelifted Punch is offered in eight trims - Smart, Pure, Pure+, Pure+ S, Adventure, Adventure S, Accomplished, and Accomplished+ S. Check out the complete price list here.

The turbo-petrol engine is available only in the mid-spec Adventure and top-of-the-line Accomplished+ S variants, priced at Rs 8.29 lakh and Rs 9.79 lakh (ex-showroom), respectively. As for the CNG + AMT combination, prices start at Rs 8.54 lakh and go all the way up to Rs 10.54 lakh (all prices ex-showroom).

The marginal price hike of Rs 9,000 to Rs 30,000, depending on the variant, feels justified given the overall improvements over the outgoing model. Compared to rivals like the Hyundai Exter, Nissan Magnite, Renault Kiger, Citroen C3, Maruti Fronx, and Toyota Urban Cruiser Taisor, the prices of the Punch are competitive. The CNG powertrain variants in the top-end are a bit pricey, but the naturally aspirated petrol and the turbo-petrol variants are well-priced. However, with almost every other car in the segment offering an automatic transmission with their turbo-petrol engine, the Punch sure seems to be at a disadvantage.

You can download the 2026 Tata Punch Facelift brochure here - Tata Punch Facelift Brochure.pdf

What's new on the outside?

Tata has been churning out some good-looking cars in recent times. The 2026 Tata Punch facelift is no different. The face has been heavily revised with some bits picked from the Punch EV and bigger Tata SUVs. The Punch facelift gets distinct styling for its bumper, radiator grille and LED DRLs:


The rear also gets a host of changes. You now get connected LED tail lamps with a sleek bar connecting the two ends, a redesigned bumper with silver inserts, along with iTurbo badging on the turbo-petrol variants. The rear camera, which was earlier placed below the logo, has also been repositioned:


Side profile remains largely unchanged, barring the new 16-inch alloy wheel design on top variants. Ground clearance has slightly gone up compared to the outgoing car (193 mm vs 190 mm):


The Punch facelift measures 3,876 mm in length (+49 mm), 1,945 mm in width (1,742 mm without mirrors) and 1,615 mm in height, which is largely the same as before. Tata is offering a bunch of new colour options on the Punch facelift - Pristine White, Bengal Rouge (Red), Coorg Clouds (Silver), Daytona Grey and Cyantafic (our test car):


We also got to drive the CNG variant, which was finished in this Caramel paint shade:


It continues to feature squared-off wheel arches and large black cladding around the car, which gives it a muscular and rugged stance:


The silver insert on the lower half of the bumper has been designed to resemble bull bars. Additionally, the radiator grille is split into two parts, with the upper part housing the front camera of the 360-degree view system:


Sleek LED DRLs look quite nice. Refreshing to see no connected DRLs on a new car. The Punch facelift comes equipped with reflector-based LED headlamps as standard. LED fog lamps with cornering function are exclusive to the top-spec Accomplished+ S variant:


The 360-degree view system is available from the mid-spec Adventure trim. The front camera for the system is neatly housed in the radiator grille:


Rain-sensing wipers are available from the mid-spec Adventure trim onwards:


Top-spec trims come with 16-inch wheels shod with 195/60 section tyres. The design of the 16-inch diamond-cut alloys bears a close resemblance to the 19-inch units seen on the Safari facelift:


While both Accomplished and Accomplished+ trims come with 16-inch wheels, the former only gets painted alloys. Meanwhile, lower variants are equipped with 15-inch wheels shod with 185/70 section tyres. Rear wheels get drum brakes as standard:


The turn indicators and side camera for the 360-degree view system are integrated into the ORVM:


Voice-assisted electronic single-pane sunroof is offered on the Pure+ S, Adventure S and Accomplished+ S trims. Black-coloured roof rails are placed on either side of the sunroof:


When the Punch was first launched, it didn’t get a shark-fin antenna. It was later introduced on certain trims, and on the facelift, it is available from the Pure+ trim onwards:


Rear defogger is offered from the Pure trim onwards. Meanwhile, the rear wiper and washer are only available on the Adventure and higher variants. It’s about time manufacturers make this a standard fitment across variants, given the added safety and convenience:


A rather simple horizontal layout for the brake light and turn indicators:


Brake and hazard lights in action:


iTurbo badging on the boot:


CNG variants get the iCNG badging:


A nice little Rhino easter egg placed on the rear windshield:



What's new on the inside?

The dashboard layout of the 2026 Tata Punch facelift is largely similar to that of the Punch EV. You get a new steering wheel, a digital driver’s display, and a touch-operated air-con control panel:


Flat-bottom, two-spoke steering wheel is borrowed from other Tata cars and only gets tilt adjustment. It has a few blank switches on the right since there's no bigger 10.25-inch fully-digital instrument cluster like the Nexon or Punch EV:


Paddle shifters are available on the top-end Accomplished + variant on the NA + AMT & CNG + AMT combinations. It allows you to take manual control and comes in handy when you need a quick downshift for overtaking:


There’s no cruise control on the top-spec Accomplished iCNG AMT variant. Tata says the feature was omitted as most customers are expected to use the car primarily within city limits. As a result, there are blank switches on the RHS of the steering wheel, which looks odd on what is the most expensive variant in the lineup:


What you get instead is a smaller 7-inch digital driver's display in the centre, accompanied by a digitised rev counter on the left, and fuel and temperature gauges on the right:


The iCNG variants get a CNG fuel gauge in place of the temperature gauge found on regular models:


You also get to see the drive mode split between petrol and CNG on the MID:


The central screen displays a host of information, including time, direction, outside temperature, odometer and DTE. Additionally, you can toggle through different menus to view instant power and torque figures, along with fuel economy data. The Punch facelift also comes equipped with passive TPMS as standard:


A few dummy switches are placed next to the engine start/stop switch. Most of the controls are now integrated into the central touch panel or the touchscreen:


The pedals have been carried over from the outgoing car and are adequately spaced. Like the outgoing model, it’s good to see that the dead pedal is well-sized and set at a comfortable angle:


Black-and-white theme on the doors remains unchanged and still looks nice:


Tata has re-engineered the seat bolstering on the Punch facelift for better overall support. These are fabric seats with a dual-tone finish:


Extended thigh support is a new addition and is offered as standard across all trims. This definitely helps in improving the underthigh support for taller occupants, especially at the front:


As a norm, the CNG variants have to be equipped with a fire extinguisher. It’s placed under the driver’s seat, and to access it, you need to push the seat all the way back: 

You’ll find a couple of places that have a big sticker reminding you of the exact location of the fire extinguisher:


This 10.25-inch touchscreen has been carried over from the outgoing car with some minor tweaks. The touchscreen looks crisp, feels smooth to operate, and we didn’t notice any lag either:


You can control various functions of the car, including the air-con settings and 360-degree view system, via the touchscreen. Speaking of the latter, the camera quality of the surround view system is quite nice, and you even get guided lines for both front and rear views:


Another Rhino easter egg. This one is placed behind the floating touchscreen:


Physical buttons for the foglamps, hazard lights, 360-degree camera, lock/unlock function, and most A/C functions have been replaced by a touch panel. These require you to take your eyes off the road while operating them. Thankfully, fan speed and temperature are still controlled by physical switches. However, the shiny gloss black finish on the panel is a fingerprint magnet:


The switch to toggle between CNG and petrol modes is placed on the central touch panel. The Punch iCNG starts in CNG mode by default, unless gas levels are low:


The wireless phone charger has a cooling fan and worked effectively in our experience. You also get a 65-watts type-c fast charging port:


Identical gear knob to the Altroz Racer and Nexon. The gearbox has a notchy feel to it, but the gates are well-defined:


The gear lever is similar to what we’ve seen on other Tata cars equipped with an AMT:


The centre console has been redesigned to accommodate a tiny movable armrest. It is ergonomically positioned, though, and comfortable over long drives:


There’s also a small storage bin under the armrest. The exposed nut doesn’t look good and seems like the base cover was missing in our test car:


Look closer, and you'll see the tri-arrow detailing, which looks quite nice:


You now get auto-dimming IRVM on top variants:


The view through the IRVM is just average. It’s mainly restricted by the headrests and the thick C-pillars:


The front tweeters are placed in the A-pillars:


Roof bezel holds the switches for the sunroof and passenger airbag warning. The top-spec Accomplished+ S variant also gets emergency call and breakdown call buttons here:


The sunroof can be opened using voice commands:


The rear seats feature the same dual-tone finish and extended thigh support as the front seats. No headrest for the central passenger. Also, the absence of load sensors in the rear seats means you have to keep the seat belts buckled in at all times. It’s high time manufacturers stop cutting costs in this area:


There isn’t much legroom available for my 5-foot-11-inch frame when seated behind my own driving position. The Punch was never the most spacious offering in its class, and the same continues with the facelift model. The rear seat is best suited for two adults and a child. The added under-thigh support does help improve comfort, though:


Rear AC vents are available from the Pure trim onwards:


You also get a single 15-watt Type-C charging port for rear passengers:


The boot space for the non-CNG variants remains unchanged at 366 litres (measured till the roof):


Like all recent Tata CNG cars, the Punch iCNG comes with twin cylinders placed below the boot floor. This frees up 210 litres of boot space (measured up to the roof), making it usable for grocery runs or a weekend outing:


As mentioned earlier, the rear camera is now placed below the tailgate. Unfortunately, it's placed right in the centre where a lot of people might hold and pull to shut the tail gate, which could result in displacing the camera over time. Hence, the warning sticker:

Driving the Tata Punch 1.2 Turbo-Petrol MT


1.2-litre, 3-cylinder turbo-petrol engine makes 118 BHP @ 5,500 rpm & 170 Nm @ 1,750 - 4,000 rpm. It is mated to a 6-speed manual gearbox:


The Punch iTurbo is powered by the same 1.2-litre three-cylinder turbo-petrol engine found in the Nexon and Altroz Racer. Like its hatchback sibling, it is offered only with a 6-speed manual gearbox. However, when you look at some of the Punch’s turbo-petrol rivals, they come equipped with a torque converter or a CVT automatic, which puts the Tata on a backfoot. When asked about this decision, Tata stated that the turbo variants are aimed at driving enthusiasts who prefer manual gearboxes. However, an automatic option could be considered if there is sufficient demand.

Press the clutch, hit the start button, and the engine comes to life smoothly. Similar to the Altroz Racer, the clutch is light to operate, but getting a smooth release takes some getting used to. It’s tricky to judge the exact bite point, and the abrupt engagement can catch you off guard, occasionally causing the car to lurch forward if you’re not careful. Release the clutch gently without any throttle input, and the Punch moves off the line smoothly.

City drivability is excellent, allowing you to putter around with minimal gearshifts. Turbo lag is well controlled, so closing traffic gaps isn’t an issue. Once the turbo spools up at ~ 1,750 rpm, there’s a noticeable surge in power, and the engine pulls strongly till ~ 4,500 rpm. We’d recommend upshifting at this point, as there isn’t much to extract from the top end of the rev range. The engine doesn’t sound particularly sporty when pushed hard either. There are no driving modes on offer with the Punch iTurbo. Throttle response, however, is well calibrated for urban driving.

The 6-speed manual gearbox does feel slightly notchy to operate. Omkar reckons it’s a noticeable improvement over earlier Tata cars, though. The throws aren’t very long, and the gear lever slots into place accurately on most occasions.

Out on the highway is where the added power of the turbo-petrol engine truly makes a difference. While the claimed 0–100 km/h sprint time of 11.1 seconds seems on the slower side for a car with a power-to-weight ratio of 105 BHP/ton, real-world performance feels more than adequate. The strong mid-range makes overtaking effortless, and the Punch iTurbo feels relaxed cruising at triple-digit speeds. The engine does 100 km/h in 6th gear at ~2,000 rpm and 120 km/h at ~2,500 rpm (it is difficult to get an accurate reading on the digital tacho meter). The engine feels relaxed while cruising, and you can do triple-digit speeds all day long.

Noise, Vibration & Harshness (NVH)

The Punch iTurbo offers a mixed-bag experience in terms of NVH. On the positive side, vibrations are well controlled for a three-cylinder engine at idle. However, the characteristic three-cylinder clatter does make its way into the cabin while on the move. You can distinctly hear the engine every time you step on the throttle. Additionally, tyre noise starts becoming noticeable beyond 80 km/h, which could get tiring during longer highway drives.

Mileage & Fuel Economy

Tata has not revealed the official fuel efficiency figures for the Punch iTurbo. As is the case with most turbo-petrol cars, fuel economy is heavily dependent on driving style. We’ll wait for Team-BHP ownership reviews for a more realistic picture of real-world efficiency.

The fuel tank capacity is 37 litres:



Driving the Tata Punch 1.2L Petrol-CNG AMT

The Punch iCNG AMT is powered by a 1.2L three-cylinder naturally-aspirated petrol engine that produces 72 BHP and 103 Nm in CNG mode, and 87 BHP and 115 Nm in regular petrol mode:


Tata first introduced the CNG + AMT combination on the Tigor, where we found it to be reasonably drivable in the city but average on the highway. The story remains largely the same with the Punch iCNG AMT. Tata’s CNG cars are unique in the sense that they start in CNG mode by default, and the Punch iCNG follows the same approach. This is uncommon, as most CNG vehicles start in petrol mode to ensure adequate lubrication before switching to the drier CNG fuel. Tata claims it has calibrated the injectors to ensure proper lubrication from the get-go, preventing any long-term harm to the engine.

The AMT gear lever offers ‘R’, ‘N’, ‘A’ and ‘M’ positions. There’s no ‘P’ (park) mode here, unlike torque converter, CVT or dual-clutch automatics. To start the engine, the gearbox must be in neutral position, with the brake pedal depressed. The car simply won’t crank if the gear lever is in any position other than ‘N’.

Lift your foot off the brake pedal, and the Punch iCNG AMT creeps forward at around 8 km/h, which makes it convenient to drive in bumper-to-bumper traffic. Press the accelerator gently, and the engine builds speed in a gradual manner, upshifting at ~ 2,500 rpm. If you floor the throttle, the gearbox holds onto gears till close to the redline (~6,000 rpm) before shifting. City drivability is more than adequate as long as you drive with a light foot. The gearbox responds reasonably well to throttle inputs and keeping pace with the traffic is not much of an issue.

You can switch between petrol and CNG modes by pressing the sweetly integrated ‘CNG’ button below the touchscreen. While shifting from petrol to CNG, the system makes a "clunk" sound, but it is muted and not as loud as some rivals, and there is no jerk felt. Also, the car will automatically switch to petrol mode in case of low CNG levels. You will notice that the throttle response is a little dull in CNG mode, yet still acceptable. Another thing you will notice while driving in CNG mode is that the air-con still manages to cool the cabin effectively. The engine's performance is further dulled though.

Out on the highway, performance is just about sufficient for relaxed cruising. At 80 km/h in fifth gear, the engine spins at roughly 2,500 rpm, but there isn’t much grunt on offer for quick overtakes at these speeds.

Like most AMTs, gearshifts take about half a second, and you do experience the typical head-nod effect at lower speeds. To smooth out the experience, it helps to lift off the accelerator slightly once you’ve reached your desired speed and want the gearbox to upshift. The ‘M’ (manual) mode can be useful when you need a quicker response for overtakes, though it requires you to operate the gear lever with your left hand.

This is where the paddle shifters come in handy. They save you the effort of reaching for the gear lever and add a layer of convenience. That said, there’s a noticeable lag between paddle inputs and the AMT’s response, which means the experience isn’t particularly engaging. You still need to plan overtakes in advance. Also, the car continues to drive in manual mode for about a minute before going back to auto mode. In case you forget to make downshifts while slowing down in manual mode, the AMT will do it for you, so as to prevent the engine from stalling.

Overall, the addition of the AMT has made the Punch iCNG more convenient for city commutes. Credit to Tata for offering the CNG powertrain in fully loaded trims. However, the top-spec Accomplished+ S variant, priced at Rs 10.54 lakh (ex-showroom), feels expensive when you consider the overall performance and driving experience on offer.

Noise, Vibration & Harshness (NVH)

Unlike the iTurbo model, the Punch iCNG exhibits a mild body shake on engine startup. You can also hear more of the engine inside the cabin at idle. Noise levels remain well controlled at city speeds, but as speeds rise, the engine starts sounding louder and coarser. Similar to the turbo-petrol variant, tyre noise becomes noticeable at highway speeds.

Mileage & Fuel Economy

The Punch iCNG is equipped with two 30-litre (water capacity) CNG tanks mounted under the boot floor. Theoretically, this should allow the tanks to hold around 10 kg of CNG at optimum pressure. In real-world conditions, however, usable capacity depends heavily on tank pressure, which reduces with regular use and drops further if the tank is frequently run low.

Tata has not yet revealed the official fuel efficiency figures for the Punch iCNG AMT. For reference, the standard Punch CNG has a claimed efficiency of 26.99 km/kg.

The micro switch placed at the top cuts off the ignition as soon as the fuel lid is opened. It also prevents the engine from starting until the lid is properly closed:


Here's a look at the twin 30-litre (water capacity) CNG cylinders. Tata has also added reinforcements at the rear to limit damage to the cylinders in the event of a rear impact:


Tools are tucked away on the side of the cylinders:



Suspension


Ride Comfort

Like the outgoing model, the Punch facelift continues with a McPherson strut setup at the front and a twist beam suspension with coil springs at the rear. As before, the ride quality feels mature with a slight hint of firmness. The suspension does an adequate job of dealing with broken city roads, though sharp bumps do make themselves felt inside the cabin. Top-end variants continue to ride on 195/60 R16 tyres, which contribute to the firmer ride quality.

As speeds increase, the suspension setup starts to shine. It offers ample confidence to tackle undulations and bumps without needing to slow down significantly. What’s also commendable is how silently the suspension goes about its job; you rarely hear a thud or clunk filtering into the cabin.

In the case of CNG models, the Punch uses a firmer set of dampers. Even while driving with the recommended tyre pressure rating, you will notice the firmness of the suspension. Yet ride quality is still compliant enough on regular roads. On the other hand, you will feel broken roads more and sharp bumps come in stronger.

Steering

The two-spoke steering wheel on the Punch facelift has been borrowed from the Nexon and feels chunky in the hand. At city speeds, the electric power steering isn’t as light as the units found on the Hyundai Exter or Maruti Suzuki Fronx. On the flip side, it weighs up nicely at highway speeds, inspiring confidence when driving at a faster pace.

Handling & Dynamics

When Omkar drove the pre-facelift Punch, he had mentioned that the chassis and suspension setup could handle more power than what the 1.2-litre NA petrol engine offered. Even with the more powerful turbo-petrol motor under the bonnet, the Punch continues to impress dynamically. Straight-line stability at highway speeds remains excellent, and the car maintains its composure well during quick direction changes.

Braking

The Tata Punch continues with a front disc and rear drum brake setup. Braking performance is progressive and more than adequate for everyday use. That said, a sharper initial bite from the brake pedal would have helped inspire more confidence. Under hard braking from higher speeds, the car comes to a halt in a straight line without any drama. There is some nose dive, but it is well controlled.

Disclaimer: Tata invited Team-BHP for the Tata Punch Facelift test drive. They covered all the travel expenses for this driving event.

Nice Review! and I am sure this will set the Sales Charts on Fire. Tata Punch is a well established brand in the market and this will only take it further.

Having said that, I went into the Comparison View on the Tata Punch website and scrolled thru fiddling around with a detailed comparison view here: Spec Compare

My Opinion:
It is well "spec-ed" car for the form factor - good choice for people wanting a lot of features at a small factor as a city run about or a first car but it does not appear to be economical in comparison to the segment.

Why do I say this: (and I am comparing only AMTs/Automatics here)
Adventure AMT: 8.14 L (9.82 L BLR) 15 inch, Cooled Glovebox, Autofold
Accomplished AMT: 8.84 L (10.65 L BLR) 16 inch + Alloy, LED DRLs, Ambient Lighting, Cooled Glovebox, Autofold, Hill Descent Control
Accomplished + S AMT: 9.54L (11.47 L BLR) 16 inch + Diamond Cut Alloy, Integrated Roof Rails, Sunroof, 7" Digital Cluster, Paddle Shifters, Rear Seat Central Armrest, Hill Descent Control, Rear Fog Lamp, 5 Tweeters

Firstly, If I want the 16 inch Variants for that true Micro SUV feel, I need to plan as a minimum for the Accomplished Variant which starts only at 8.84 L and a good 10.65L On Road BLR.

Interestingly I noticed Tata does not offer even the Hill Descent Control on variants below this which come with 15 inchers. Not even Roof Rails or an Armrest - Sorry.

Then for all the gloss they are showing on the Media Drives including the Sunroof and Paddle Shifters, it would go all the way till 9.54L Ex Showroom, 11.47 L OTR BLR - this I think is on the higher side for a NA Petrol + AMT Combo.

At this price point, the better choices in my view would be:
Renault Kiger Turbo CVT (9.14 L / 11.13 L BLR) or the Magnite Acenta HRAO 1.0 Turbo Petrol CVT (9.14L / 11.16 L BLR) which would give you a 100 BHP Turbo Petrol mated to a CVT and a slightly larger car with more room. And the Kiger also comes with a segment leading boot of 405 L and the Nissan Magnite boasts a 5 Star Rating from GNCAP. You will not a get a sunroof with these two though.

Yes, Tata is a bigger brand with all-India reach and larger service network (though we keep saying its hit or miss). And of course, you would need to do some homework on service center availability etc. if you are in a non-metro but for someone in a metro - I think the value of the Renault-Nissan twins is undeniable in comparison. With the launches being announced around Duster and Tekton these two are not leaving anytime soon.

Although if Factory Fitted CNG AMT is your choice - look no futher - the Tata Punch is the best bet there.
